setting.definition: The act of putting something in place.
setting.example: Setting boundaries gets harder as children grow older.

ignorant.example: When I was 14, I thought my father was so ignorant.

astonished.example: I was astonished at how much my father had learned.

habit.definition: Something you do regularly, often without thinking.
habit.example: It is easier if you are already in the habit of defining yourself.

involved.example: He was involved in a supermarket sweep.

supermarket.definition: A large shop that sells food and other everyday things.
supermarket.example: They tried to steal from the supermarket.

sweep.definition: In 'supermarket sweep': rushing through a shop grabbing items quickly.
sweep.example: A group filled a trolley in a supermarket sweep.

trolley.definition: A wheeled cart used for carrying things in a shop.
trolley.example: They filled a trolley with beer and sweets.

dash.example: They tried to make a dash for it.

sweets.example: The trolley was full of beer and sweets.

swept along.definition: Carried forward by a group without thinking about it.
swept along.example: He said he just got swept along with it all.

character.definition: The kind of person someone is; their personality and values.
character.example: This was totally out of character for him.

snapshot.definition: A quick picture of something at one moment in time.
snapshot.example: Beer and sweets is a snapshot of where a teenager is at.

halfway.definition: In the middle between two things.
halfway.example: A teenager is halfway between childhood and adulthood.

confusing.definition: Hard to understand; making you feel mixed up.
confusing.example: Being a teenager is very confusing.

disappointed.definition: Sad because something did not happen as you hoped.
disappointed.example: Saying you feel disappointed stings more than insults.

stings.example: The word 'disappointed' stings more than calling them an idiot.

mortified.example: I felt mortified when I had to pick him up from the police station.

criminals.example: Individually, you do not appear to be natural criminals.

dare.definition: To challenge someone to do something risky or difficult.
dare.example: They started to dare each other.

egg each other on.definition: To encourage each other to do something, often something risky.
egg each other on.example: When you boys get together, you egg each other on.

peer pressure.definition: The strong feeling that you must do what your friends are doing.
peer pressure.example: Peer pressure between the boys was hard to resist.

resist.definition: To fight against something and not give in to it.
resist.example: The peer pressure was hard to resist.

brakes.definition: In 'put the brakes on': to stop or slow down something.
brakes.example: How are you going to put the brakes on next time?

consequences.definition: The results of an action, especially bad results.
consequences.example: They could still have a laugh without the terrible consequences.

detention.definition: A punishment where you must stay at school after classes end.
detention.example: Maybe you were already angry because of the detention.

rebellious.definition: Refusing to follow rules and wanting to fight against authority.
rebellious.example: He might have been feeling angry and rebellious.

grounded.definition: Punished by not being allowed to go out.
grounded.example: Instead of saying you are grounded, define yourself.

labelling.definition: Describing someone with a word that puts them in a category.
labelling.example: Labelling them as untrustworthy will not help them improve.

incapable.example: Don't label your child as incapable.

impulsive.definition: Acting quickly without thinking about what will happen.
impulsive.example: Calling them impulsive does not help.

untrustworthy.definition: Not able to be trusted or relied on.
untrustworthy.example: Calling your teen untrustworthy damages the relationship.

immature.definition: Not fully grown up in behaviour or thinking.
immature.example: Don't label your teenager as immature.

stubbornness.definition: The quality of refusing to change your mind.
stubbornness.example: A punitive approach only increases stubbornness.

enhancing.definition: Making something better or improving it.
enhancing.example: Punishment goes no further in enhancing understanding.

responsibly.definition: In a careful and sensible way.
responsibly.example: You can probably behave responsibly on the bus.

modelling.definition: Showing by example how to behave.
modelling.example: By being honest, you are modelling honesty.

tolerant.definition: Able to accept things that are different or difficult without complaining.
tolerant.example: They may be more tolerant of your decision.

transport.definition: A system for moving people from one place to another (buses, trains, etc.).
transport.example: Taking public transport alone is a big step for a teenager.
